text: The robotic system is docked, and the surgeon enters the abdomen through the established ports.
The camera is oriented inferiorly, providing a view towards the patient's feet.
The left iliac artery is highlighted in red, visible within the surgical field.
The left iliac vein is highlighted in blue, visible in the surgical field.
A segment of the bowel, highlighted in orange, is visibly hanging down in the pelvic region.
The surgeon sweeps the bowel cephalad, moving it away from the pouch of Douglas, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The bladder is visible in the surgical field, with the mobile dome being grasped and stretched by the surgeon.
The Foley catheter balloon is visibly deflated within the surgical field.
The surgical team prepares to make the first incision, using visible lab marks on the patient's skin as a guide.
The surgeon incises the peritoneal reflection at the level of the vas deferens, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon traces the vas deferens proximally towards the seminal vesicle, carefully freeing it from the surrounding tissue using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon shifts focus to the lateral sides of the prostate after freeing the seminal vesicles and prostate base from the Denonvilliers' fascia.
The surgeon uses a combination of sharp dissection and minimal electrocautery to carefully separate the prostatic tissue from its adjacent neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon performs the same dissection maneuver on the contralateral side of the prostate, using a combination of sharp dissection and minimal electrocautery to separate the prostatic tissue from its neurovascular bundle.
The surgeon continues the dissection caudally, reaching the prostatic apex while preserving the neurovascular bundles on either side of the prostate.

Following meticulous lateral dissection into the prostatic apex, the surgeon shifts focus proximally to the junction between the bladder and prostate, preparing for further surgical intervention at this anatomical site.
The surgeon dissects the bladder at its distal extent beyond the trigone, carefully avoiding the ureteral orifices by following the bladder's funneling into the prostatic urethra.
The bladder fibers appear as longitudinal and crisscrossing bundles, contrasting against the smooth surface of the prostatic capsule.
The surgeon gently teases the neurovascular bundles off the prostatic base, ensuring adequate margins are maintained during the dissection.
The surgeon incises the posterior lip of the prostatic urethra at the bladder's narrowest portion, revealing the Foley catheter within the bladder lumen.
The video shows a clear demarcation between the prostate posteriorly and the bladder attached anteriorly, highlighting the anatomical separation between these structures.
The inset displays the surgical maneuver being performed using the da Vinci robotic system, showcasing precise manipulation of the bladder and prostate structures.
The surgeon has successfully freed the prostate from its proximal attachment to the bladder neck, revealing the separation between these anatomical structures.
The prostate remains attached to the distal urethra, with visible separation from other surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully teases away the fibers surrounding the urethroprostatic junction, revealing the elongated urethra.
The surgeon sharply cuts the urethra, revealing fresh, viable mucosa intended for later approximation.
The surgeon cuts the remaining fibers and rolls the prostate off the prostatic fossa, completely freeing it from surrounding attachments.
The inset displays the final steps of the prostate removal using the da Vinci robotic system, showcasing the complete detachment of the prostate from surrounding structures.
The surgeon places the freed prostate into a specimen bag using the da Vinci robotic system.
The surgeon inserts the lap sac through the twelve millimeter assist port, pulls it shut, and deflects it towards the left paracolic gutter, removing it from the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to commence the vesicular urethral anastomosis, positioning the surgical field for the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon employs the Yonsei technique, utilizing a barbed suture with two needled ends for the vesicular urethral anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the anterior anastomosis using continuous stitches, with the sutures visibly securing the tissue edges together.
The surgeon performs mucosa-to-mucosa approximation of the bladder to the urethra, ensuring precise alignment of the tissue edges.
The surgeon threads the Foley catheter into the bladder lumen, ensuring it is fully inserted.
The surgeon inflates the Foley catheter balloon after completing the posterior stitches of the vesicular urethral anastomosis.
The surgeon performs the Yonsei technique by suturing from inside to outside on both the upper and lower edges of the anastomosis, carefully avoiding damage to the catheter.
The surgeon ties the ends of the thread at the six o'clock position on the anastomosis, securing the suture line.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal reflection using a single layer of barb suture after achieving hemostasis.
The surgeon positions a Jackson-Pratt (JP) drain in the Douglas pouch, ensuring proper placement for postoperative fluid drainage.
The surgeon positions the string of the specimen bag into the surgical field, making it visible.
The camera is transferred to the assist port, and the string is pulled out of the optical port for specimen retrieval through the umbilicus.
